title: Trimming a thrown bowl at leather-hard
subtitle: Re-centring an upturned leather-hard bowl on the wheel and cutting a foot ring with a loop tool
excerpt: Trimming is the second wheel session for any thrown piece. The leather-hard bowl is inverted on the wheel head, re-centred by tapping, and a foot ring is carved from the surplus clay at the base. This tutorial covers the trimming workflow for a smooth stoneware bowl of around 500-700 g; including how to tell when the clay is at the right stage, how to centre by ear and eye, and how to set the foot ring proportions.
sourceNotes: Trimming technique derived from first principles and standard wheel-thrown practice. Encyclopaedia Britannica, eleventh edition (1911), pottery entry. V&A open-access collection for the historical-form reference on foot ring proportions across English stoneware traditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim, join, and carve. The trimming window.
A recessed ring trimmed into the base of a pot that lifts the work off the kiln shelf and refines the base profile.
Unfired clay in any drying stage.
Air-dried but unfired clay. Too late for trimming; the loop tool tears bone-dry clay rather than cutting it.

content.text: Trimming is the second wheel session for a thrown piece. The bowl, having stiffened to leather-hard since the throw, is inverted on the wheel head, re-centred by tapping while the wheel turns slowly, and the surplus clay at the base is carved away to leave a recessed foot ring. The foot ring lifts the bowl off the kiln shelf, refines the base profile, and reduces the total weight of the finished piece. A potter's wheel is required; no kiln work is involved at this stage.
When to trim
The clay must be at
leather-hard

; stiff enough to invert without distorting, soft enough that a loop tool cuts a clean curl of clay rather than tearing crumbs. Test by pressing a fingernail into the bottom of the bowl: it should leave a mark without releasing slip, and without resistance feeling like wood.

On a standard studio day, a freshly thrown 500-700 g bowl reaches the trimming stage in 8-24 hours, covered loosely with plastic for the first few hours and then uncovered. Too soft and the bowl deforms when inverted; too dry and the loop tool tears. The window is forgiving for a couple of hours either side; it closes hard once the clay reaches
bone-dry
, at which point the trimming opportunity is gone and the base stays as thrown.
Setting up
Wipe the wheel head clean and dry. Have a needle tool, a loop tool, and a damp sponge within reach. The bowl will be inverted on the wheel head; no bat is needed for trimming.
Inverting and centring the bowl
1. Place the bowl upside-down on the wheel head

Lift the bowl carefully (supporting the rim from below) and turn it upside-down. Place the rim on the centre of the wheel head, eyeballing the centre as best as possible. The rim is now the contact surface; the wheel turns slowly underneath.
2. Spin slowly and tap into centre

Start the wheel at the slowest setting. Place the index finger of one hand against the outside of the bowl base, holding it at a fixed position. Watch the gap between the finger and the spinning bowl. The bowl will be off-centre to begin with; the gap opens and closes as the bowl rotates.

When the bowl rotates to its widest point (the gap closes), gently tap the bowl with the side of the other hand or a folded sponge; toward the centre, not the edge. Each tap nudges the bowl a small amount toward the centre. Several taps over half a minute centre the bowl. The gap stops opening and closing; the bowl is centred.
3. Secure the bowl to the wheel head

Once centred, press three or four small coils of fresh clay around the rim of the bowl onto the wheel head. The coils anchor the bowl in place; without them the bowl shifts under the loop tool pressure and goes off-centre again. The coils press lightly enough that they release without damaging the bowl rim.
Marking and cutting the foot ring
1. Mark the foot ring diameter with the needle tool
Spin the wheel at medium-low speed. Hold the needle tool steady against the upturned base, perpendicular to the wheel. Touch the needle to the base at the desired outer diameter of the
foot ring
; typically 15-20 mm in from the outer wall on a bowl. The needle scratches a clean circle. Mark the inner edge of the foot ring the same way, 6-8 mm inside the outer mark.
2. Carve the outer slope

Hold the loop tool at a 45-degree angle to the bowl base, the loop touching the outer edge of the base. Press lightly and let the spinning wheel feed the clay to the cutting edge of the loop. The loop carves a curl of clay away; the outer profile of the bowl flows from the wall down to the foot ring outer line. Continue until the outer edge of the foot ring stands proud and the clay below it slopes inward to the foot ring.
3. Cut the foot ring recess

Hold the loop tool flat against the base inside the inner foot ring line. Press lightly and let the wheel feed clay to the cutting edge. Carve the recess to a depth of 4-5 mm; enough to make the foot ring visible without weakening the base. Test the floor thickness by pressing the inside of the bowl down with one hand; the floor should feel solid, not flex. If the floor flexes, the recess is cut too deep and the bowl will warp in the bisque firing.
4. Bevel the foot ring edges

Hold the loop tool at a 30-degree angle and pass it lightly along the outer edge of the foot ring, then the inner edge. Both bevels remove the sharp corner. A sharp foot ring chips during handling and can scratch the kiln shelf; the bevelled ring is more durable and sits cleaner on a finished surface.
Finishing

Stop the wheel. Brush loose trimmings off the bowl with a soft brush. Smooth any tool marks on the foot ring and recess with a damp sponge passed lightly over the cut surfaces. Release the anchor coils; lift the bowl off the wheel head; turn the bowl right-side up onto a board. The trimming is done. The bowl now dries to
bone-dry
over the next several days, then can be loaded for bisque firing.
What it looks like when it is right

A trimmed bowl sits on its foot ring without rocking. The foot ring is concentric with the outer wall; the inner recess is clean and even in depth. The outer profile of the bowl flows continuously from the rim down through the wall, around the bowl curve, and onto the foot ring outer slope without a visible step. The bowl weight has reduced by roughly 15-20% compared to the wet thrown weight; a 500 g wet bowl weighs roughly 400-425 g after trimming. The remaining
greenware
has clean tool marks where the loop passed; these soften on the bisque firing and are usually preserved as decorative texture.
Common mistakes
Trimming clay that is too soft. The bowl distorts as soon as the loop tool presses on it. Wait several hours and check the leather-hard stage again.

Trimming clay that is too dry. The loop tool tears clay in crumbs rather than carving a clean curl. If a piece has dried past trimming, spritz the surface with water, wrap in plastic for two to four hours, and check again; usually the surface re-softens enough to trim.

Cutting the foot ring recess too deep. The base flexes when pressed, which means the bowl will warp in firing and may collapse. Cut conservatively; check the floor thickness by squeezing between two fingers (one inside the bowl, one in the recess) and aim for 5-6 mm of floor under the foot ring at minimum.
Foot ring too narrow. A foot ring under 4 mm wide cracks under the weight of the pot and may chip off in the bisque firing. Trim to a working 6-8 mm width on functional ware.
Diagram note

A cross-section diagram of a trimmed bowl shows several lines. The bowl wall flows from rim to base. The outer side slopes inward from the wall down to the outer edge of the foot ring. The foot ring stands 4-5 mm proud and 6-8 mm wide. The inner recess cuts into the base 4-5 mm deep, leaving a floor 5-6 mm thick. The bevels at both edges of the foot ring soften the corners.

items.symptom: Bowl wobbles after centring on the wheel
items.cause: Anchor coils pressed too hard on one side, knocking the bowl off-centre
items.fix: Re-centre by tapping; press anchor coils evenly with light pressure on all four points

items.symptom: Loop tool catches and chatters across the surface
items.cause: Clay too dry, or tool angle too steep
items.fix: Mist the surface with water. Wrap in plastic for an hour to soften it. Hold the loop at a flatter angle (20-30 degrees) so the cutting edge skims rather than digs

items.symptom: Foot ring is off-centre (visible asymmetry after trimming)
items.cause: Bowl was not centred properly before anchoring
items.fix: Spend longer at the tapping stage; the gap between a stationary finger and the spinning bowl edge must not open and close before anchor coils go on

items.symptom: Pot cracks at the base after trimming during drying
items.cause: Floor cut too thin in the foot ring recess, or trimming was done before centring fully
items.fix: Leave 5-6 mm of clay between the inside of the bowl floor and the bottom of the recess; cover the trimmed bowl with plastic for the first 12 hours to slow drying
